label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the main idea of this passage. The question is asking what the passage is mainly about. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I'll read through the passage again to get a good grasp. The passage starts by talking about London's subway system and its historical value, but the main point seems to shift when it discusses the behavior of commuters. It mentions that people in London avoid eye contact and are busy with their own activities. Then it goes into detail about how they use technology—like smartphones (iPhones, iPods) and apps to pass the time. There's a specific mention of Apple products and the number of apps designed since the iPhone's launch. The author is highlighting how the way people spend their time on the subway has changed due to technology. The key points are the shift from quiet reflection or reading books to using electronic devices. The examples given (apps like iShoot, Tube Exits, iSteam, iPods, and palm DVD technology) all support the idea that technology is influencing commuters' activities. Now looking at the options: A) London commuters are unfriendly to strangers. The passage mentions avoiding eye contact but clarifies it's not rudeness, just busyness. So this is more about behavior than unfriendliness. Probably not A. B) Apple has earned a lot from selling 40,000 apps. While the passage does mention Apple and 40,000 apps, that's just one part of the discussion. It's an example supporting the main idea, not the main idea itself. So B is probably too narrow. C) Technology is changing how London commuters spend their traveling time. This seems to cover the various examples given (apps, iPods, DVDs) and the overall theme that technology has replaced older habits. This seems like the main idea. D) Riding a London
